era_context: The High Middle Ages; the investiture controversy continues between the papacy and the Holy Roman Empire. In Iberia, the Reconquista advances. The Byzantine Empire is stabilizing under Alexios I Komnenos. In East Asia, the Song Dynasty rules China; Japan is in the late Heian period. The Islamic world is experiencing significant political fragmentation, particularly in the Seljuk Empire.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia beyond Islamic sources (limited documentation)

events.title: Assassination of Nizam al-Mulk, Seljuk vizier
events.description: Nizam al-Mulk, the powerful vizier of the Great Seljuk Empire under Sultan Malik Shah I, is assassinated on October 14, 1092, by an Ismaili assassin (likely associated with the Nizari Ismailis). Nizam al-Mulk had been the architect of Seljuk administrative and military organization for decades and was one of the most influential figures in the Islamic world. His death removes a stabilizing force from the empire and contributes to the rapid fragmentation that will follow Malik Shah's death just weeks later. The assassination is a watershed moment in Seljuk history and marks the beginning of the empire's decline.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Nizam al-Mulk's assassination

events.certainty_note: The assassination of Nizam al-Mulk on October 14, 1092, is well-attested in Islamic sources. The date is one of the most securely dated events of this period.
cross_references: 1095-002 (First Crusade, indirect consequence of Seljuk fragmentation)

events.title: Consecration of Durham Cathedral
events.description: Durham Cathedral is consecrated on August 4, 1092, in the presence of King William II Rufus of England. The cathedral, begun in 1093 (or possibly earlier), is one of the finest examples of Norman Romanesque architecture and represents the power and wealth of the Norman church in England. The consecration marks a significant moment in English ecclesiastical history and the consolidation of Norman religious authority in the north of England. The cathedral will become one of the most important pilgrimage sites in medieval England, housing the shrine of Saint Cuthbert.
key_figures: Bishop William of Saint-Calais (Bishop of Durham)

sources.name: Historia Ecclesiastica by Bede (earlier chronicle, referenced in later sources)

events.certainty_note: The consecration date of August 4, 1092, is given in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, though some sources place the beginning of construction in 1093. The exact chronology of the cathedral's construction and consecration is debated by modern scholars.

events.title: Zhenzong of Song China dies; succession of Huizong
events.description: Emperor Zhenzong of the Northern Song Dynasty dies on March 23, 1100 (or possibly 1092 in some sources, though 1100 is more likely). [Note: This entry is uncertain due to conflicting dates in sources. The traditional date for Zhenzong's death is 1100, not 1092. If the year 1092 is correct, it may refer to another event in Song history.] The succession of a new emperor marks a transition in Song politics. However, the exact year and nature of this succession require clarification from more reliable sources.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government, 11th-century Chinese chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The dating of Song imperial successions is uncertain in this research. Zhenzong's death is traditionally dated to 1100, not 1092. This entry is included with low confidence and should be verified against more reliable Chinese sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Durham Cathedral's consecration is disputed: the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle gives August 4, 1092, but some sources suggest the cathedral was begun in 1093 and consecrated later. The exact chronology of the cathedral's construction is debated. The entry on Song Dynasty succession is highly uncertain and may be misdated; Zhenzong's death is more reliably dated to 1100. The significance of Nizam al-Mulk's assassination has sometimes been overstated in Western historiography as a direct cause of the Crusades, though it did contribute to Seljuk fragmentation.
historiographic_note: The year 1092 is moderately well-documented in Western European sources (English chronicles) and Islamic sources (particularly for Nizam al-Mulk's assassination). Chinese sources for this period are available but may not be well-represented in Western-language historiography. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Islamic sources are often fragmentary and focus on dynastic politics. The biases of literate elites shape the surviving record.

graph_edges.note: Nizam al-Mulk's assassination preceded and contributed to Malik Shah's death and Seljuk fragmentation
Durham Cathedral became a major pilgrimage site in medieval England
